journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Cancer. Journal International du Cancer

title-group.article-title: GESTATIONAL AGE AT BIRTH AND RISK OF TESTICULAR CANCER

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Medicine, Stanford University, Stanford, CA
2Center for Primary Health Care Research, Lund University, Malmö, Sweden
3Stanford Prevention Research Center, Stanford University, Stanford, CA
4Department of Health Research and Policy, Stanford University, Stanford, CA

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding author: Casey Crump, M.D., Ph.D., 900 Blake Wilbur Drive, Stanford, CA 94304-2205, Tel: (650) 723-6963, Fax: (650) 498-7750, kccrump@stanford.edu

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

abstract.p: Most testicular germ cell tumors originate from carcinoma in situ cells in fetal life, possibly related to sex hormone imbalances in early pregnancy. Previous studies of association between gestational age at birth and testicular cancer have yielded discrepant results and have not examined extreme preterm birth. Our objective was to determine whether low gestational age at birth is independently associated with testicular cancer in later life. We conducted a national cohort study of 354,860 men born in Sweden in 1973–1979, including 19,214 born preterm (gestational age <37 weeks) of whom 1,279 were born extremely preterm (22–29 weeks), followed for testicular cancer incidence through 2008. A total of 767 testicular cancers (296 seminomas and 471 nonseminomatous germ cell tumors) were identified in 11.2 million person-years of follow-up. Extreme preterm birth was associated with an increased risk of testicular cancer (hazard ratio 3.95; 95% CI, 1.67–9.34) after adjusting for other perinatal factors, family history of testicular cancer, and cryptorchidism. Only five cases (three seminomas and two nonseminomas) occurred among men born extremely preterm, limiting the precision of risk estimates. No association was found between later preterm birth, post-term birth, or low or high fetal growth and testicular cancer. These findings suggest that extreme but not later preterm birth may be independently associated with testicular cancer in later life. They are based on a small number of cases and will need confirmation in other large cohorts. Elucidation of the key prenatal etiologic factors may potentially lead to preventive interventions in early life.

award-group.funding-source: National Institute of Child Health & Human Development : NICHD

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Testicular cancer is the most common malignancy among young adult men, with a peak incidence occurring at age 30 years.1–2 The large majority of testicular cancers originate from carcinoma in situ cells during fetal life.3–8 The mechanism is unknown but is thought to involve a complex interplay of estrogen/androgen imbalances in utero,9–10 likely modulated by genetic factors11 as well as other perinatal environmental exposures.12 Cryptorchidism is a well-established congenital risk factor but accounts for fewer than 10% of testicular cancers,13 so the etiology of most cases is still unknown. Elucidation of other perinatal etiologic factors is of great interest because of the potential to lead to preventive interventions in early life.

Preterm birth is associated with altered sex hormone levels prenatally and postnatally in the offspring,14–20 and therefore has been hypothesized to be a potential risk factor for testicular cancer. A number of studies have reported an association between low gestational age at birth and testicular cancer in later life,21–27 although others have failed to confirm this.28–32 The discrepancy in these results may be due in part to ascertainment or publication bias among studies that were based on self-reported data.33 The studies to date have also had other limitations, including the inability to examine the separate effects of preterm birth and low fetal growth, or to examine the risk of testicular cancer in men who were born extremely preterm.

We conducted a national cohort study in Sweden to examine whether low gestational age at birth, independent of fetal growth, is associated with testicular cancer in later life. All men who were born in Sweden in 1973–1979 were followed for testicular cancer incidence through 2008. We hypothesized that low gestational age at birth is independently associated with an increased risk of testicular cancer in later life.

sec.title: Study population
sec.p: We identified 357,775 males in the Swedish Medical Birth Registry who were live-born from 1973 through 1979. We excluded 1,526 (0.4%) males who had missing information for gestational age at birth, and 252 (0.1%) who had missing information for birth weight. In order to remove possible coding errors, we also excluded 1,137 (0.3%) who had a reported birth weight more than six standard deviations above or below the mean birth weight for gestational age and gender from a Swedish reference growth curve.34 A total of 354,860 males (99.2% of the original cohort) remained for inclusion in the study. This study was approved by the Ethics Committee of Lund University in Malmö, Sweden.

sec.title: Testicular cancer ascertainment
sec.p: The study cohort was followed for testicular germ cell tumor incidence from birth through December 31, 2008 (maximum attained ages ranged from 29 to 36 years). All testicular germ cell tumors, including seminomas and nonseminomatous germ cell tumors (embryonal carcinomas, yolk sac carcinomas, choriocarcinomas, teratomas, and mixed germ cell tumors) were identified using the Swedish Cancer Registry. This registry includes all primary incident cancers in Sweden since 1958, with compulsory reporting nationwide.

sec.title: Gestational age at birth ascertainment
sec.p: Gestational age at birth was based on maternal report of last menstrual period, identified from prenatal and birth records in the Swedish Birth Registry.35–40 In the current analyses, gestational age at birth was categorized into five groups to allow for a non-linear response (22–29 weeks, 30–34 weeks, 35–36 weeks, 37–42 weeks, ≥43 weeks). Cutpoints were chosen in order to have sufficient numbers in each category for statistical analysis.

sec.title: Other study variables
sec.p: Other perinatal and demographic characteristics that may be associated with the subsequent risk of testicular cancer were identified from the Swedish Birth Registry and national census data. An anonymous personal identification number was used to link information from parents to their sons. The following were included as adjustment variables:

sec.title: Birth year
sec.p: Modeled as a continuous variable.

sec.title: Fetal growth
sec.p: Measured as the number of standard deviations from the mean birth weight for gestational age and gender from a Swedish reference growth curve,34 categorized into six groups (<−2 SD; −2 SD to <−1 SD; −1 SD to <0 SD; 0 SD to <1 SD; 1 SD to <2 SD; ≥2 SD).

sec.title: Multiple birth status
sec.p: Singleton or twin. Included because twinning is associated with preterm birth and low fetal growth, and may be associated with an increased risk of testicular cancer in the male offspring.33

sec.title: Birth order
sec.p: 1, 2, or ≥3. Included because first pregnancy is associated with low birth weight or small for gestational age,41 and also has been associated with an increased risk of testicular cancer in the male offspring.12

sec.title: Mother’s and father’s age at birth
sec.p: <20 years, 20–24 years, 25–29 years, 30–34 years, or ≥35 years; entered into the model separately for mothers and fathers. Some studies have reported that low maternal or paternal age,42 or high maternal age,28 is associated with testicular cancer in the male offspring.

sec.title: Mother’s and father’s education level
sec.p: Compulsory high school or less (≤9 years), practical high school or some theoretical high school (10–11 years), or theoretical high school and/or college (≥12 years); entered into the model separately for mothers and fathers.

sec.title: Family history of testicular cancer in the father or a brother
sec.p: Yes or no; identified from the Swedish Cancer Registry from 1958 through 2008, and entered into the model separately for fathers and brothers.

sec.title: Cryptorchidism
sec.p: Yes or no; identified from the Swedish Birth Registry and the Swedish Hospital Discharge Registry. This information was used to assess whether an association between gestational age at birth and testicular cancer was mediated by cryptorchidism.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Cox proportional hazards regression was used to estimate hazard ratios (HRs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the association between gestational age at birth and testicular cancer in later life. Individuals were censored at emigration (n=17,858; 5.0%) or death (n=8,013; 2.3%). Analyses were conducted first unadjusted and then adjusted in two different models. Adjusted model 1 included the following variables: gestational age at birth, birth year, fetal growth, multiple birth, birth order, mother’s and father’s age at delivery, mother’s and father’s education level, and family history of testicular cancer in the father or a brother (see variable definitions above). Adjusted model 2 included the same set of variables as well as cryptorchidism. Robust standard errors were used in all models to account for correlation among siblings. A likelihood ratio test was used to test for equality of testicular cancer hazard functions among different gestational age groups. The proportional hazards assumption was evaluated with a global test and separate tests for each gestational age stratum using the method described by Grambsch and Therneau.43

We also explored first-order interactions between gestational age at birth and each of the model covariates with respect to testicular cancer, using a likelihood ratio test to evaluate for statistical significance. All analyses were conducted using Stata statistical software, version 11.0.44

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: Of the 354,860 individuals in this cohort, 19,214 (5.4%) were born prematurely (<37 weeks), including 1,279 (0.4%) born at 22–29 weeks, 5,967 (1.7%) born at 30–34 weeks, and 11,968 (3.4%) born at 35–36 weeks. Testicular germ cell tumors were observed in 767 (0.2%) men, including 296 seminomas and 471 nonseminomas, in 11.2 million person-years of follow-up. Cryptorchidism was reported for 2.1% of men in the entire cohort, and was slightly more common among those born moderately or late preterm. A total of 5.1% of men with testicular cancer during the study period had a history of cryptorchidism, compared to 2.0% of those without testicular cancer (data not shown). A family history of testicular cancer in a brother was more common among men born preterm than those born full- or post-term (Table 1).

After adjusting for demographic/perinatal factors and family history (adjusted model 1), extreme preterm birth (22–29 weeks) was associated with an increased risk of testicular cancer (HR 4.20; 95% CI, 1.76–10.0), particularly seminomas (HR 6.90; 95% CI, 2.18–21.9). These estimates were based on only five testicular cancers (three seminomas and two nonseminomas) among men born extremely preterm, resulting in wide confidence intervals. Further adjustment for cryptorchidism had little effect on these risk estimates (HR 3.95 [95% CI, 1.67–9.34] for all testicular cancers and HR 6.48 [95% CI, 2.01–20.8] for seminomas). No association was found between later preterm birth or overall preterm birth and testicular cancer (Table 2). A modest, non-significant association was observed between post-term birth (≥43 weeks) and testicular cancer (fully adjusted HR 1.41 [95% CI, 0.97–2.05]), based on 31 cases among men who were born post-term. A likelihood ratio test showed that the testicular cancer hazard functions by gestational age group were significantly different (P=0.02).

Among the covariates in the fully adjusted model, the only significant predictors of testicular cancer were birth year (HR 1.05; 95% CI, 1.01–1.09), family history of testicular cancer in either the father (HR 3.00; 95% CI, 1.12–8.03; P=0.03) or a brother (HR 8.77; 95% CI, 2.17–35.4; P=0.002), and cryptorchidism (HR 2.50; 95% CI, 1.81–3.47; P<0.0001) (Table 3). Neither low nor high fetal growth was associated with testicular cancer, with or without adjustment for gestational age at birth and the other covariates.

The only significant first-order interaction was between gestational age at birth and paternal education level (P=0.003), but the difference in risk estimates across different paternal education strata were not in a consistent direction and did not appear clinically meaningful. No interaction was found between gestational age at birth and fetal growth (P=0.30) or multiple birth status (P=0.56). Sensitivity analyses showed that excluding twins did not meaningfully change any results. We also explored for an interaction between low birth order and high maternal age since this was previously reported,21 but none was found (P=0.51).

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: These findings from a large national cohort suggest that extreme but not later preterm birth may be associated with testicular cancer in later life. This association appeared to be independent of fetal growth as well as other perinatal factors, family history of testicular cancer, and cryptorchidism. In contrast, we found no association between later or overall preterm birth, or low or high fetal growth, and testicular cancer. The precision of risk estimates for men born extremely preterm was limited due to the small number of these men who have survived to young adulthood and the rarity of testicular cancer. Confirmation of these findings in future cohort studies will be needed as larger numbers of men who were born extremely preterm survive into adulthood. In addition, we found a modest, non-significant association between post-term birth and testicular cancer that also will need further evaluation with extended follow-up and greater statistical power.

Previous studies of association between preterm birth or low birth weight and testicular cancer have yielded inconsistent results. A recent meta-analysis reported an overall odds ratio of 1.31 (95% CI, 1.07–1.59) for the association between low gestational age at birth and testicular cancer, and 1.34 (95% CI, 1.08–1.67) for the association between low birth weight and testicular cancer.33 However, these estimates were driven mainly by studies based on self-reported data and were not confirmed by most registry-based studies. This discrepancy may be due in part to ascertainment bias in studies relying on self-reported testicular cancer, or possibly publication bias among those studies.33 Our study is consistent with most previous registry-based ones in finding no association between overall preterm birth and testicular cancer, but is the first to examine extreme preterm birth.

In contrast to some earlier studies, we found that testicular cancer was not associated with twinning,33 low birth order,12 low maternal or paternal age,42 or high maternal age.28 Our results confirmed the well-documented associations between family history of testicular cancer or cryptorchidism and testicular cancer. In addition, we found a birth cohort effect, with a 5% increasing risk of testicular cancer by each later year of birth from 1973 to 1979 (P=0.02). This is consistent with but somewhat larger than the birth cohort effect previously reported for Sweden during 1983–1997.1 These birth cohort effects, combined with invariant age-incidence effects noted across many European countries, suggest the presence of one or more important environmental determinants acting prenatally that have yet to be identified.1–2

The large majority of testicular cancers originate from carcinoma in situ cells in utero.3–8 The exact mechanism is unknown but appears to be highly complex and mediated in part by estrogen/androgen imbalances.9 High maternal levels of either androstenedione or estradiol in early pregnancy have been associated with an increased risk of testicular cancer.10 Preterm birth and low birth weight are associated with early and lifelong alterations in glucocorticoids and sex hormones, which have strong immunomodulatory effects.14–20 Extreme preterm birth may be associated with testicular cancer either as a proxy for in utero exposures during the critical period of testicular differentiation, or itself may directly modify the risk of testicular cancer postnatally.

The current study has several strengths and limitations. It is the first to examine the association between extreme preterm birth and testicular cancer in later life. An important strength was the ability to examine this association in a large national cohort by linking birth and cancer registry data, thus avoiding bias that may result from self-reporting. Other registry information on cryptorchidism, family history of testicular cancer, and perinatal factors enabled the evaluation of these factors as potential confounders or mediators. A limitation was the estimation of gestational age by maternal report of last menstrual period rather than by ultrasound, which was not yet widely used at the time these men were born (1973–1979). In addition, this cohort was followed to a maximum age of 36 years, limiting ascertainment particularly of seminomas which have a peak incidence at approximately this age, as opposed to nonseminomas which peak about a decade earlier.45

In summary, this national cohort study suggests that extreme but not later preterm birth may be independently associated with testicular cancer in later life. These findings were based on a small number of cases among men born extremely preterm, and will need additional follow-up and confirmation as larger numbers of these men survive into adulthood. Elucidation of the key etiologic factors acting prenatally may potentially lead to preventive interventions in early life.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
ack.p: This study was supported by grants from the National Institute of Child Health and Human Development [1R01HD052848-01], the Swedish Research Council [2008–3110 and 2008–2638], the Swedish Council for Working Life and Social Research [2006–0386, 2007–1754, and 2007–1962], and ALF project grant, Lund, Sweden. The funding agencies had no role in the design and conduct of the study; in the collection, analysis, and interpretation of the data; or in the preparation, review, or approval of the manuscript.

fn.p: There were no conflicts of interest for any authors.

Novelty and impact: This national cohort study is the first to examine the association between extreme preterm birth and risk of testicular cancer, which may be related to sex hormone imbalances in early pregnancy. The findings suggest that extreme but not later preterm birth may be independently associated with testicular cancer in later life.

caption.p: Testicular cancer, cryptorchidism, and family history of testicular cancer by gestational age at birth (1973–1979)

td.bold: History of testicular cancer in the father
History of testicular cancer in a brother

caption.p: Hazard ratios for association between gestational age at birth (1973–1979) and testicular cancer (through 2008)

fn.p: Adjusted for birth year, fetal growth, multiple birth, birth order, mother’s and father’s age at birth, mother’s and father’s education level, and family history of testicular cancer in the father or a brother.
Adjusted for the same variables as above, as well as cryptorchidism.

caption.p: Adjusted hazard ratios* for association between model covariates and testicular cancer

tr.td: Practical high school or some theoretical high school (10–11)
Practical high school or some theoretical high school (10–11)

td.bold: History of testicular cancer in the father
History of testicular cancer in a brother

fn.p: The model included gestational age at birth, birth year (as a continuous variable), fetal growth, multiple birth, birth order, mother’s and father’s age at birth, mother’s and father’s education level, family history of testicular cancer in the father or a brother, and cryptorchidism. The reference category for all categorical variables is indicated by a hazards ratio of 1.00.
